paper_title: Engaging Overseas Sri Lankans to Facilitate Export Diversification
research_answer: Both Taiwan and China experienced significant waves of diaspora return that were closely tied to the build-out of their technology export sectors, but the scale and timing differed. Taiwan saw 40,000 Chinese return from the US between 1986 and 1996, driven partly by a US technology recession in the early 1990s. This wave was critical to establishing Taiwan's high-tech export identity — by 2000, 42% of founders in the Hsinchu science park were diaspora returnees, and Taiwan had captured 90% of global scanner production. China's return wave came slightly later: by 2002, the Chinese news agency reported 130,000 diaspora had returned in a phenomenon dubbed 'B2C' (back-to-China). The much larger scale of China's return — over three times Taiwan's figure — reflects both China's larger population base and the intensity of the post-dot-com-crash reverse migration. Together, these cases suggest that external shocks in advanced economies (recessions, tech busts) can accelerate diaspora return and catalyze export complexity upgrading in origin countries.
supporting_quotes: facing a US technology recession at the beginning of the 1990s, 40,000 Chinese returned to Taiwan from the US between 1986-1996
By the year 2000, 42% of company founders located in the Hsinchu science park (the home of the Taiwanese high-tech sector) were diaspora returnees
China too saw many return in a phenomenon dubbed B2C (back-to-China), the Chinese news agency at one point in 2002 reported that 130,000 diaspora had returned
by 2000 Taiwan produced 90% of the world's scanners, with Microtek itself producing 20% of the market
